content:
# Numbers Chapter 17 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pages 169-170 | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/NUM_017_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/NUM_017_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pa_0125-0126 | **Source Images:** `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/NUM_017_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/NUM_017_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

> **Note on Versification:** This chapter follows the Masoretic (Hebrew) versification, in which Numbers 17 contains twenty-eight verses. In English Bibles, MT 17:1-15 corresponds to 16:36-50, and MT 17:16-28 corresponds to 17:1-13. Both sections are translated here as a single chapter following the Hebrew source.

---

### The Holy Censers Recovered (vv. 1-5)

**1** And YHWH spoke to Moses, saying,

**2** "Tell Eleazar son of Aaron the priest to pick up the censers from the midst of the burning, and scatter the fire far away, for they have become holy —

**3** the censers of these sinners at the cost of their lives. And let them be made into hammered plates as a covering for the altar, for they presented them before YHWH, and they became holy. And they shall be a sign to the Sons of Israel."

**4** And Eleazar the priest took the bronze censers which those who were burned had presented, and they hammered them out as a covering for the altar,

**5** as a reminder to the Sons of Israel, so that no outsider, who is not of the seed of Aaron, should come near to burn incense before YHWH, so that he would not become like Korah and his congregation — as YHWH had spoken to him by the hand of Moses.

---

### The Plague upon the Congregation (vv. 6-15)

**6** And the entire congregation of the Sons of Israel grumbled on the next day against Moses and against Aaron, saying, "You have killed the people of YHWH."

**7** And it came to pass, when the congregation assembled against Moses and against Aaron, that they turned toward the Tent of Meeting, and behold, the cloud covered it, and the glory of YHWH appeared.

**8** And Moses and Aaron came to the front of the Tent of Meeting.

**9** And YHWH spoke to Moses, saying,

**10** "Get away from the midst of this congregation, and I will consume them in a moment." And they fell on their faces.

**11** And Moses said to Aaron, "Take the censer and put fire upon it from the altar, and place incense on it, and carry it quickly to the congregation and make atonement for them, for wrath has gone out from before YHWH; the plague has begun."

**12** And Aaron took it, as Moses had spoken, and ran into the midst of the assembly, and behold, the plague had already begun among the people. And he placed the incense and made atonement for the people.

**13** And he stood between the dead and the living, and the plague was checked.

**14** And those who died by the plague were fourteen thousand and seven hundred, besides those who died on account of Korah.

**15** And Aaron returned to Moses at the entrance of the Tent of Meeting, and the plague was checked.

---

### YHWH Commands the Staff Test (vv. 16-20)

**16** And YHWH spoke to Moses, saying,

**17** "Speak to the Sons of Israel, and take from them a staff, a staff for each father's house, from all their leaders according to their fathers' houses — twelve staffs. Write each man's name upon his staff.

**18** And write the name of Aaron upon the staff of Levi, for there shall be one staff for the head of each of their fathers' houses.

**19** And you shall lay them in the Tent of Meeting before the testimony, where I meet with you.

**20** And it shall be that the man whom I choose, his staff shall bud; and I will cause to cease from upon me the grumblings of the Sons of Israel, which they grumble against you."

---

### The Staffs Are Laid Before YHWH (vv. 21-23)

**21** And Moses spoke to the Sons of Israel, and all their leaders gave him a staff, one staff for each leader, according to their fathers' houses — twelve staffs. And the staff of Aaron was among their staffs.

**22** And Moses laid the staffs before YHWH in the Tent of the Testimony.

**23** And it came to pass on the next day that Moses went into the Tent of the Testimony, and behold, the staff of Aaron for the house of Levi had budded; it had put forth buds and produced blossoms and bore ripe almonds.

---

### The Sign Displayed and the Staff Preserved (vv. 24-26)

**24** And Moses brought out all the staffs from before YHWH to all the Sons of Israel, and they looked, and each man took his staff.

**25** And YHWH said to Moses, "Put back the staff of Aaron before the testimony, to be kept as a sign for the sons of rebellion, that you may put an end to their grumblings against me, so that they do not die."

**26** And Moses did so; as YHWH had commanded him, so he did.

---

### The Cry of Israel (vv. 27-28)

**27** And the Sons of Israel said to Moses, saying, "Behold, we perish! We are lost! All of us are lost!

**28** Everyone who comes near, who comes near to the Tabernacle of YHWH, dies. Shall we ever cease perishing?"

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Textual Notes

1. **Verses 1-5 / English 16:36-40 — Chapter division:** The Masoretic tradition begins chapter 17 at what English Bibles number as 16:36. This is not arbitrary: the Masoretes understood the recovery of the censers and the subsequent plague as the beginning of a new literary unit — the vindication of Aaron's priesthood — rather than as the conclusion of Korah's rebellion. The Leningrad Codex clearly marks the *parashah* division here. The LXX (Vaticanus) does not follow this division, treating the material as continuous with the preceding narrative.

2. **Verse 2 — "scatter the fire far away" (וְאֶת־הָאֵשׁ זְרֵה־הָלְאָה):** The imperative *zereh* ("scatter") indicates that the coals from the censers must be dispersed, separating the holy vessel from the profane fire. The Vaticanus reads *to pur to allotrion* ("the alien/strange fire"), adding an interpretive layer not present in the MT. I follow the Leningrad Codex.

3. **Verse 3 — "at the cost of their lives" (בְּנַפְשֹׁתָם):** Literally "by/with their souls." The preposition *be-* here carries instrumental or price force: they consecrated the censers through their own deaths. The LXX renders *en tais psuchais autōn* ("in their souls"), preserving the Hebrew construction closely.

4. **Verse 5 — "outsider" (אִישׁ זָר):** Hebrew *ish zar* — literally "a strange man" — is a technical term denoting anyone outside the Aaronic priestly line. This is not ethnic foreignness but cultic exclusion: even a Levite (like Korah) is *zar* with respect to the incense-burning priesthood. The LXX renders *allogenēs* ("one of another race/kind").

5. **Verse 11 — "wrath has gone out" (יָצָא הַקֶּצֶף):** The Hebrew *qetzef* ("wrath") is here personified almost as a force unleashed from the divine presence. Aaron's incense-bearing run into the plague is a priestly counter-charge: the same ritual action that destroyed the two hundred and fifty now becomes the instrument of deliverance.

6. **Verse 13 — "between the dead and the living" (בֵּין הַמֵּתִים וּבֵין הַחַיִּים):** One of the most striking images in the Torah. Aaron stands as a physical barrier between death's advance and the living congregation. The LXX (Vaticanus) faithfully renders *ana meson tōn tethnēkotōn kai ana meson tōn zōntōn*. The spatial language is not metaphorical — Aaron's body, bearing the incense, forms the boundary that halts the plague.

7. **Verse 17 — "a staff, a staff for each father's house" (מַטֶּה מַטֶּה לְבֵית אָב):** The repetition of *maṭṭeh* ("staff, staff") is characteristic of Hebrew distributive syntax — "one staff per father's house." The LXX smooths this to *rhabdon mian kat' oikon patriōn*. I preserve the Hebrew doubling to maintain the cadence of the source.

8. **Verse 20 — "shall bud" (יִפְרָח):** From the root *p-r-ḥ*, meaning "to sprout, bud, blossom." This sets up the dramatic fulfillment in verse 23, where three stages of growth are described — buds, blossoms, and ripe almonds — all appearing overnight, far exceeding the single "budding" that was promised.

9. **Verse 23 — "had budded...put forth buds...produced blossoms...bore ripe almonds" (פָּרַח וַיָּצֵץ צִיץ וַיִּגְמֹל שְׁקֵדִים):** The Leningrad Codex preserves a remarkable sequence of four verbal stages: (1) *paraḥ* — "it budded"; (2) *wayyatzetz tzitz* — "it put forth buds/blossoms" (cognate accusative); (3) *wayyigmol* — "it bore ripe fruit"; (4) *sheqedim* — "almonds." The progression from bud to blossom to ripe fruit in a single night signals unmistakable divine intervention. The LXX reads *exēnegken blaston kai exēnthēsen anthē kai ebastēsen karua* — "brought forth shoots and bloomed flowers and bore nuts" — collapsing some stages. I follow the MT's fuller description.

10. **Verse 25 — "sons of rebellion" (בְּנֵי־מֶרִי):** Literally "sons of rebellion" or "sons of contentiousness." This phrase is a Hebrew idiom characterizing persistent quality — those defined by their rebellious nature. The LXX renders *huioi anētheis* or *tōn apeithōn* ("sons of the disobedient"). I retain the literal Hebrew idiom, which carries greater force than the softened "rebels."

11. **Verse 27 — "We perish! We are lost! All of us are lost!" (גָּוַעְנוּ אָבַדְנוּ כֻּלָּנוּ אָבָדְנוּ):** The threefold cry intensifies with each clause: *gava'nu* ("we expire/perish"), *avadnu* ("we are lost/destroyed"), *kullanu avadnu* ("all of us are lost"). The repetition of *avad* with the addition of *kullanu* ("all of us") conveys escalating communal panic. The LXX renders the sequence with similar intensity.

12. **Verse 28 — "Everyone who comes near, who comes near" (כֹּל הַקָּרֵב הַקָּרֵב):** The doubling of *ha-qarev* ("the one who comes near, who comes near") mirrors the obsessive fear now gripping Israel. After the ground swallowed Korah's company and fire consumed the incense-bearers and a plague killed fourteen thousand and seven hundred, the people are terrified that any approach to the sacred space means death. This sets the stage for the priestly legislation of chapter 18 (MT chapter numbering), where the responsibilities and boundaries of the Levitical system are defined precisely to address this fear.

### Theological and Literary Observations

- **The vindication of Aaron:** The entire chapter forms a chiastic argument for Aaronic authority. It opens with the recovery of censers that testify to the danger of unauthorized priestly approach (vv. 1-5), moves through Aaron's life-saving intercession during the plague (vv. 6-15), and culminates in the miraculous budding of Aaron's staff (vv. 16-26). The structure demonstrates that Aaron's priesthood is not self-appointed but divinely attested through three signs: the bronze-covered altar, the halting of the plague, and the living staff.

- **The staff as a sign of life:** Among all the staffs laid before YHWH, only Aaron's produces life — buds, blossoms, and ripe almonds. The Hebrew word for almond (*shaqed*) is a wordplay on *shoqed* ("watchful"), the same wordplay found in Jeremiah 1:11-12, where YHWH declares, "I am watching (*shoqed*) over my word to perform it." Aaron's almond-bearing staff thus signifies YHWH's watchful, life-giving purpose within the priesthood.

- **From rebellion to terror:** The chapter traces a psychological arc in Israel — from arrogant accusation ("You have killed the people of YHWH," v. 6) to abject terror ("Shall we ever cease perishing?" v. 28). The people's final cry is not repentance but panic, and it is this unresolved existential dread that necessitates the priestly ordinances of the following chapter. YHWH's answer to their fear is not punishment but structure: a priesthood that mediates between the holy and the common, protecting the people from the lethal holiness of the divine presence.

- **Three stages of botanical miracle:** The overnight progression from bud to blossom to ripe almond is botanically impossible under natural conditions. Almond trees (*Prunus dulcis*) require months to move through these stages. The compression of an entire growing season into a single night is the narrative's way of asserting that this sign comes from outside the natural order — it is an act of *beri'ah*, of creation, echoing the "new thing" YHWH created when the earth swallowed Korah (16:30/MT 17:30).

- **The preserved staff:** YHWH commands that Aaron's staff be placed "before the testimony" (v. 25) as a permanent sign. Alongside the jar of manna (Exod 16:33) and the tablets of the covenant, this budding staff becomes one of the sacred objects housed within or before the Ark — a living testimony preserved in the holiest space, according to later tradition (cf. Heb 9:4).

---

*Translated by LOGOS from the Leningrad Codex (B19A), pages 169-170, with reference to Codex Vaticanus (LXX), pa_0125-0126. All twenty-eight verses of Numbers chapter 17 (Masoretic versification) rendered in formal equivalence, preserving the syntax, theological vocabulary, and literary structures of the Hebrew source text.*
